raw_text: L Rumpf. Extremus labor: Vergils 10. Ecloge und die Poetik der Bucolica. Göttingen 1996. Rev: Maleuvre, LEC 6 (1998) 169-170. Van Sickle argues that Vergil's "program" in Bucolic 1 revises Theocritus' Idyll 7 and proclaims a new bucolic departure. Landscape, postures, sorts of musical art, and names have traditional, quasi-mythical status. V. uses these mythemes to fashion a new poetic myth, with several analogues and two programmatic plots. Henderson's study of Eclogue 9 is rib-tickling and perceptive.

AAC 9928410 Heckel's study of the function of military and political language in the Georgics sheds fresh light on the contexts and implications of the verse treatise on agriculture. McDonough juxtaposes Vergil's Georgics and the Book of Revelation to good effect. Llewelyn Morgan's Patterns of Redemption continues to win enthusiastic responses from the critics. Sabine Schafer' s study assesses the role of Lucretius in the development of Vergil's world view.

Mazzocchini's analysis of the war narrative in the epic, with the lists of the dead, is thorough and provocative. Perutelli's "profile" of Eduard Norden' s impressive scholarship on the epic portrays the savant in his contemporary world. Ricottilli' s study of gesture and word expands Highet's magisterial study of the speeches in Vergil. Ernst Schmidt's articles, particularly on "American and German Approaches," a veritable "battlefield" of contrasting approaches and views, strains to accent the humanitas and comprehensive nature of Vergil's Weltgedicht. "He wrote the Aeneid to bring to light fundamental questions of humanity, foremost among them the relation and antagonism of state and empire, that is, power and historical mission, on the one hand; and on the other, human happiness, that is, a life free of sacrifice, suffering, and guilt" (171). Victor Udwin Vergilian Bibliography 2000-2001 201 moves audaciously as champion of comparative and interdisc nary approaches to texts. In his view, "In combination, the term 'epic' and 'culture' will refer specifically to the 'understandi according to which that practice would have been meaningful" Texts, for Udwin, served to transmit and reinforce "understandin specifically, the acceptability of the duel under certain circu stances. Adkin accents accedo (Aen. 1 . 1 95-202), with its suggestion of "attachment and alliance," and accersit at Aen. 5.746, implying "assistance," as another instance of Vergil's etymologizing craft. Brucia argues that Harpalyce, who surfaces in the Venus episode at Carthage, ominously conflates the essence of two women: a fast-tracking warrior princess, and a rape-victim, a child-killer metamorphosed into a bird. Celaeno, the Harpy, provides another awesome party, and a measure of word-play in the Strophades. Abbott is concerned with Coroebus' stratagem (2.370-434) and his defence of "just deceit." In fact, the recurring nexus of dolus, arma, and civil strife (furor) reveals that dolus is malus. Anderson and Davis shed light on the vexed tradition of the Wooden Horse: both articles are noteworthy for their learning and innovative approach. Scafoglio reviews the controversial Helen episode and argues for ultrici flamma at 587 as preferable to ultricis flammae. Clement's article highlights the Breughelesque that surfaces in Vergil's epic. Konstan organizes Catullus (66.39-41), A en. 6.460, Callimachus, Sophocles' Antigone , and testor/testa, to resolve the pun. Smith suggests that Nero's Decursio sestertii (ca. A.D. 62-63) depict a Lusus Troiae squadron reminiscent of Aen. 5.545-605. Bleisch brings sympathetic Vergilian Bibliography 2000-2001 203 insights to the Deiphobus figure and Aeneas' encounter with him Hades. Jeanne Dion introduces Gallus into her study and sugges that Misenus may be his symbolic twin. AENEID: INDIVIDUAL BOOKS 7-12

raw_text: A. Traina. L'utopia e la storia. Il libro xu dell' Eneide e antologia delle opere. Torino, 1997. Rev: Landolfi, Lexis 17 (1999) 398-404. The ecphrasis of the Shield of Aeneas is a perennial topic. Scully's approach is novel: he detects destructive elements of fire and passion transmuted from savagery to victory flames and images. But, the conversion is incomplete, because traces of base metal remain. Fowler and Fucecchi shed complementary light on the Nisus and Euryalus episode in the ninth book. Fowler also treats the significance of the depiction on Pallas' baldric (Aen. 10.495-99 and 12.940-52) under "The Ruin of Time: Monuments and Survival at Rome," in his Roman Constructions (see item 204 below). Jacobson suggests that the proper name Ia-pyx, when aligned with Greek pyxis "medicine box," befits the healer and his drugs. According to Beare, Vergil's common swallow (Juturna's counterpart at Aen. 12.473-78) gathered up flies, small edibles, suitable for nestlings. Casali shows that Idmon, Turnus' messenger to Aeneas on the eve of the duel, is indebted to the prophet who participates in the Argonauti expedition, certain of death. So Vergil suggests that Turnus' recourse to Idmon signals his awareness of the same inevitability. Nicoli perceives that the near disaster for Aeneas, wounded on the field, and its reversal, signals the necessity of Turnus' death and guarantees Aeneas' progress towards apotheosis (12.793-94). Toptsi accents the penitential and retributive quality of Turnus' death, and the poet's concern with the preventive/providential factor. "It is an act of restraint planned ultimately by Jupiter and executed by the hand of Aeneas, although ignorant of the cosmic significance of his Vergilian Bibliography 2000-2001 205 act, which aims at securing the stability of the social order Aen struggles to establish in Italy" (177).

raw_text: A. M. Keith, Engendering Rome: Women in Latin Epic. Roman Literature and Its Contexts. Cambridge, 2000. Rev: Gale, G&R 48 (2001) 95; Damien Neliš, Vergilius 46 (2000) 192-196. Binder's study of the reception of the Dido-tragedy is comprehensive and directional. Burden's anthology of essays contains a welcome account of Elizabethan Dido (Purkiss) a dazzling gallery attesting to Dido's presence in the visual arts (Montagu), a fresh interpretation of Mercury's double visit (Feeney), and a search for metatheatrical elements in Dido's tragedy (Pobjoy). Drake's essay points out that the connection with the Punic Wars in book 4 is pervasive and that Vergil's sympathetic portrayal of Dido is part of a complex reading of Rome's rise to power and a demonstration that the struggle with Carthage was woven into the same fate by which Romans had been destined to rule the world. Drake argues that book 4 overall is an allegory of the three Punic Wars, and a pessimistic interpretation of recent events; the fall of the Republic and the destruction of Carthage were interconnected .

Critics have returned to terra firma in their review of Roman monuments and their application to Vergil's texts. Diane Favro's study of The Urban Image has excited lynx-eyed scrutiny of Augustan Rome by students and scholars, and has evoked some illuminating reviews of her "trial" venture. raw_text: J. Wills. Repetition in Latin Poetry. Figures of Allusion. Oxford, 1996. Rev: Dangel, Latomus 59 (2000) 149-152. Vergilian metrics have regained critical attention as support for character and context. Farrell's study of Latin Language and Latin Culture offers a different wide-ranging view of Latin as a language and as a vehicle for cultural practice: Vergilians should welcome this important examination of Vergil's medium. Laird's study of speech presentation offers valuable testimony and acute criticism, and can be profitably associated with Lobe's perceptive study. Putnam's study of the nature and role of ekphraseis in the epic offers a summary challenge to past and future understanding of their implications for the epic. Richard Thomas' essays on intertextuality in Vergil offer instructive and cogent "new" directions for Vergilian scholars. Wills continues to excite appreciative critical response to his cogent study of repetition and figures of allusion.

raw_text: John Warden. " Patria Praecepta : Lucretius and Vergil in the Underworld." Vergilius 46 (2000) 83-92. Breed targets echo ( resonare ) as a phenomenon of the fictional world, and as metaphor for relations between texts. Vergil's Bucolics are traditional, but echo allows him to sidestep literary chronology. "The Bucolics can be read alongside, not strictly after, his modern texts." Gale's exploration of Vergil's indebtedness to resonances and instruction in Lucretius' didactic poem is welcome and authoritative. Giesecke' s study of cross-generic imitation of Lucretius' poem treats a formative factor in Vergil's art and complements Gale's work. Warden's study of patria praecepta examines the "dialogue" involving Aen. 6.719-853 and DRN 3.1024-1094 where Vergil disputes with (and overturns) Lucretius on many counts.
